spec_match.rationale: Applies font-variant-emoji at the chat surface level — exactly the product-wide consistency use case the spec was designed for.

interactive.rationale: Dropdown flips the variant on every bubble, plus composer input lets the visitor type new messages and see them render under the chosen variant.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Card promises a live chat composer with product-wide variant — page delivers.

edge_cases.rationale: No demo of what happens with `font-variant-emoji: normal` on a Linux box without an emoji font (the platform default would fall back). No demo of inheritance — if a child bubble sets its own variant, does it win? Implied but not shown.

references.rationale: See also has no chromestatus.com link. Rule #3 regression.

design_system.rationale: Bubble styles via CSS variables; no static-card pattern.

openQuestions.title: Add the chromestatus.com reference link
openQuestions.detail: Rule #3 self-heal: https://chromestatus.com/feature/6566092561973248 must be in see-also.

openQuestions.title: Demonstrate per-message override
openQuestions.detail: A real chat surface might pin `font-variant-emoji: emoji` to user messages but `text` to system messages. Add a 'system message' bubble with its own override so visitors see the inheritance/override interplay.

openQuestions.title: Show how platform default differs across OS
openQuestions.detail: Add a 'simulated platform' selector (macOS / Windows / Linux-no-emoji-font) that mocks the platform default rendering. Helps the visitor understand WHY consistency matters across the install base.

summary: Convincing product-context demo with real composer interaction. Missing the chromestatus reference link and an over-platform-defaults comparison that would land the motivation.
